message.content:
Adversarially review implemented task T140 (goal G11, defect D40). Return the structured verdict JSON {taskId, verdict:"approve"|"disapprove", criticism:[], questions:[], defects:[], rationale, summary} and WRITE NOTHING to the ledger.

Fetch acceptance: `mcp__ledger__fetch_item` ledger=tasks item=T140.
Branch implement/T140 at commit d23b940c9f71f75a441eb4f2c70617a8d09ef4bd, based at main 180ac3e. Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a36797e065c1f835a.
Diff: `git -C /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia diff 180ac3e d23b940c9f71f75a441eb4f2c70617a8d09ef4bd`.
Worker summary: D40 reconciled — Linux >=5.7 SO_BINDTODEVICE needs NO capability for a not-yet-bound socket; empirically confirmed on o3.7mind.io (6.17.0-1018-oracle) via a C probe as uid 65534 'nobody' with zero caps (capsh Current: =) succeeding. Updated pathsock_linux.go + pathsock.go comments, both systemd units, docs/install.md; CapabilityBoundingSet unchanged (CAP_NET_ADMIN-only).

Independently verify: (1) grep -rn CAP_NET_RAW internal/ packaging/ docs/ leaves no contradicting claim; all four surfaces (pathsock_linux.go, both units, docs/install.md) state the same >=5.7-qualified rule with the source-IP EPERM fallback; (2) the diff is comment/docs-ONLY and neither unit's CapabilityBoundingSet was widened; (3) the >=5.7 kernel-relaxation claim is correct (the probe as uid nobody/zero-caps actually proves NO capability needed, consistent with the comment) and the supported-distro floor is >=5.7; (4) nothing functional changed. Run `cd /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a36797e065c1f835a && nix develop -c sh -c 'go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...' && nix develop -c just lint`. Split criticism[] vs questions[]; file genuine defects. Be adversarial about whether the comment overclaims vs the probe evidence.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
diff --git a/docs/install.md b/docs/install.md
index 6ee56af..74f2e08 100644
--- a/docs/install.md
+++ b/docs/install.md
@@ -871,6 +871,16 @@ systemctl enable --now wanbond-edge      # or wanbond-concentrator
 - The units run with `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` (TUN creation) plus
   standard hardening. If you set `wireguard.listen_port` below 1024, add
   `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` to the bounding set.
+- Device-bind paths (`bind = "auto"` or `"device"`, `SO_BINDTODEVICE`) need no
+  further capability on Linux >=5.7: since kernel commit c427bfec18f21 ("net:
+  core: enable SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users"), binding a not-yet-bound
+  socket to a device requires no capability at all — empirically confirmed
+  (D40) with `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` unchanged. Pre-5.7 kernels
+  need `CAP_NET_RAW`, which these units deliberately don't grant; on such a
+  kernel device-bind fails with EPERM and the daemon transparently falls back
+  to source-IP binding (see [install.md §3b](#3b-policy-routing-edge-topologies-source-ip-pinning-with-bind--source)
+  for when that fallback matters). All kernels this project supports
+  (Debian bookworm 6.1+, Ubuntu 22.04 5.15+) are already >=5.7.
 
 ### NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in
 
diff --git a/internal/bind/pathsock.go b/internal/bind/pathsock.go
index bbdc134..c8b09a0 100644
--- a/internal/bind/pathsock.go
+++ b/internal/bind/pathsock.go
@@ -28,10 +28,12 @@ import (
 // no other path contends for the device (see selectDeviceBinds). When dev is ""
 // the socket pins the specific source IP — the pre-T16 behaviour.
 //
-// Device binding is also BEST-EFFORT even when selected: SO_BINDTODEVICE needs
-// CAP_NET_RAW (the daemon runs privileged, but the unit tests bind loopback
-// unprivileged) and is Linux-only, so a device-bind failure falls back to
-// source-IP binding rather than failing Open.
+// Device binding is also BEST-EFFORT even when selected: on Linux <5.7,
+// SO_BINDTODEVICE needs CAP_NET_RAW (the daemon runs privileged, but the unit
+// tests bind loopback unprivileged); on >=5.7 it needs no capability at all
+// (see bindToDevice, pathsock_linux.go, D40). It is also Linux-only, so a
+// device-bind failure falls back to source-IP binding rather than failing
+// Open.
 func listenPath(src netip.Addr, port uint16, dev string) (*net.UDPConn, error) {
 	if dev != "" {
 		if c, err := listenOnDevice(src, port, dev); err == nil {
diff --git a/internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go b/internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go
index 76c31b9..d0eb13e 100644
--- a/internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go
+++ b/internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go
@@ -6,8 +6,14 @@ import "syscall"
 
 // bindToDevice pins the socket to the named interface via SO_BINDTODEVICE, so its
 // egress and ingress are confined to that device regardless of the source address
-// currently assigned to it. It requires CAP_NET_RAW; a permission error is
-// returned to the caller, which falls back to source-IP binding.
+// currently assigned to it. On Linux >=5.7 (kernel commit c427bfec18f21, "net:
+// core: enable SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users") this needs no capability at
+// all for a not-yet-bound socket, which is the only case this daemon exercises;
+// empirically confirmed (D40) with SO_BINDTODEVICE succeeding under a
+// zero-capability, non-root process on a 6.17 kernel. Pre-5.7 kernels still
+// require CAP_NET_RAW. Either way, a permission error (EPERM, on a pre-5.7
+// kernel lacking that capability) is returned to the caller, which falls back
+// to source-IP binding.
 func bindToDevice(fd uintptr, dev string) error {
 	return syscall.SetsockoptString(int(fd), syscall.SOL_SOCKET, syscall.SO_BINDTODEVICE, dev)
 }
diff --git a/packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service b/packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service
index a8b424b..8dc629f 100644
--- a/packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service
+++ b/packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service
@@ -27,6 +27,10 @@ RestartSec=2s
 # The daemon needs only CAP_NET_ADMIN (TUN device creation); it does no
 # interface addressing or routing itself (see docs/install.md). Add
 # CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE here only if wireguard.listen_port is below 1024.
+# Device-bind sockets (SO_BINDTODEVICE, bind = "auto"/"device") need no extra
+# capability here: on Linux >=5.7 SO_BINDTODEVICE requires none at all for a
+# not-yet-bound socket (pre-5.7 needs CAP_NET_RAW, and its absence falls back
+# to source-IP binding — see internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go, D40).
 CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN
 DeviceAllow=/dev/net/tun rw
 NoNewPrivileges=yes
diff --git a/packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service b/packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service
index e4d85b8..dfbfbe2 100644
--- a/packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service
+++ b/packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service
@@ -20,7 +20,11 @@ Restart=on-failure
 RestartSec=2s
 
 # The daemon needs only CAP_NET_ADMIN (TUN device creation); it does no
-# interface addressing or routing itself (see docs/install.md).
+# interface addressing or routing itself (see docs/install.md). Device-bind
+# sockets (SO_BINDTODEVICE, bind = "auto"/"device") need no extra capability
+# here: on Linux >=5.7 SO_BINDTODEVICE requires none at all for a not-yet-bound
+# socket (pre-5.7 needs CAP_NET_RAW, and its absence falls back to source-IP
+# binding — see internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go, D40).
 CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN
 DeviceAllow=/dev/net/tun rw
 NoNewPrivileges=yes

content.text: {"item":{"id":"T140","milestoneId":"M47","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Reconcile the SO_BINDTODEVICE capability story: pathsock comment vs CAP_NET_ADMIN units vs docs (D40)","description":"internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go (~:9-10) claims bindToDevice 'requires CAP_NET_RAW', yet the shipped systemd units (packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service ~:24 + the concentrator twin) grant only CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN — and device-bind SUCCEEDED on o3, so the comment and the unit disagree (D40). (1) Determine the real requirement: historically SO_BINDTODEVICE needed CAP_NET_RAW; Linux ≥5.7 allows it with no capability (verify the kernel commit/version via WebSearch and against the supported floor — Debian bookworm 6.1+, Ubuntu 22.04 5.15+, both ≥5.7). (2) EMPIRICALLY confirm on at least one standing worker (o3 aarch64 or llm-ubuntu-0 amd64, via `ssh -i /run/agenix/llm-ssh-key`): run a minimal setsockopt(SO_BINDTODEVICE) probe (or the daemon) under a CAP_NET_ADMIN-only bounding set and observe device-bind succeed; capture the command + output + `uname -r`. (3) Align all surfaces to the finding: the pathsock_linux.go bindToDevice comment (state the ≥5.7 rule + that the EPERM fallback covers pre-5.7 kernels), any other CAP_NET_RAW mention in internal/bind, the capability comment in BOTH packaging/systemd units, and docs/install.md's capability text. Do NOT widen the unit CapabilityBoundingSet unless the probe proves CAP_NET_RAW is actually required on a supported kernel. content.content:
docs(D40): reconcile SO_BINDTODEVICE capability story across pathsock/units/install.md

Empirically confirmed on o3.7mind.io (kernel 6.17.0-1018-oracle, aarch64):
SO_BINDTODEVICE succeeds for a not-yet-bound socket under a zero-capability,
non-root process (uid 65534 "nobody", Current capability set empty). This
matches upstream kernel commit c427bfec18f21 ("net: core: enable
SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users"), landed in Linux 5.7 (June 2020): binding
an unbound socket to a device needs no capability at all on >=5.7; only a
rebind of an already-bound socket still requires CAP_NET_RAW. All kernels this
project supports (Debian bookworm 6.1+, Ubuntu 22.04 5.15+) are already >=5.7.

Update the bindToDevice comment (pathsock_linux.go), the listenPath BEST-EFFORT
comment (pathsock.go), both systemd unit files' capability comments, and
docs/install.md's systemd-units section to state the kernel-version-qualified
rule (>=5.7 needs nothing, <5.7 needs CAP_NET_RAW) with the EPERM -> source-IP
fallback documented. CapabilityBoundingSet is left unchanged in both units
(still CAP_NET_ADMIN only) since the probe shows CAP_NET_RAW is not actually
needed on any supported kernel.

Probe: ssh -i /run/agenix/llm-ssh-key ubuntu@o3.7mind.io, gcc-compiled C probe
opening a UDP socket and calling setsockopt(SO_BINDTODEVICE, "lo") as uid 65534
(sudo -u nobody), with `capsh --print` confirming Current: = (no capabilities
held). Output: "SO_BINDTODEVICE(lo) SUCCEEDED". Also reproduced running
unelevated as the ambient ubuntu user (no ambient capabilities either).

toolUseResult.stdout:

SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users"), landed in Linux 5.7 (June 2020): binding
an unbound socket to a device needs no capability at all on >=5.7; only a
rebind of an already-bound socket still requires CAP_NET_RAW. All kernels this
project supports (Debian bookworm 6.1+, Ubuntu 22.04 5.15+) are already >=5.7.

Update the bindToDevice comment (pathsock_linux.go), the listenPath BEST-EFFORT
comment (pathsock.go), both systemd unit files' capability comments, and
docs/install.md's systemd-units section to state the kernel-version-qualified
rule (>=5.7 needs nothing, <5.7 needs CAP_NET_RAW) with the EPERM -> source-IP
fallback documented. CapabilityBoundingSet is left unchanged in both units
(still CAP_NET_ADMIN only) since the probe shows CAP_NET_RAW is not actually
needed on any supported kernel.

Probe: ssh -i /run/agenix/llm-ssh-key ubuntu@o3.7mind.io, gcc-compiled C probe
opening a UDP socket and calling setsockopt(SO_BINDTODEVICE, "lo") as uid 65534
(sudo -u nobody), with `capsh --print` confirming Current: = (no capabilities
held). Output: "SO_BINDTODEVICE(lo) SUCCEEDED". Also reproduced running
unelevated as the ambient ubuntu user (no ambient capabilities either).

input.command:
uname -r; capsh --print 2>/dev/null | grep -i '^Current'; python3 -c '
import socket
s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
s.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, 25, b"lo\0")  # SO_BINDTODEVICE=25
print("SO_BINDTODEVICE(lo) SUCCEEDED unprivileged")
'

input.description: Check other doc surfaces for capability claims

content.text:
All verification complete. Compiling the verdict.

**Verification evidence:**

1. **Kernel claim** — Commit `c427bfec18f21` ("net: core: enable SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users", Vincent Bernat, merged v5.7) matches my knowledge of the upstream history; the qualified rule (unbound socket needs nothing on >=5.7, rebind/pre-5.7 needs CAP_NET_RAW) is stated correctly. I additionally reproduced the probe **independently** on this host: kernel 7.0.8, `capsh --print` → `Current: =` (zero capabilities), and `setsockopt(SO_BINDTODEVICE, "lo")` on a fresh UDP socket succeeded. Combined with the worker's o3 probe (6.17.0-1018-oracle, uid nobody), the no-capability claim holds on two kernels. Distro floors (bookworm 6.1, Ubuntu 22.04 5.15) are both >=5.7 — correct.
2. **Comment/docs-only** — every hunk in `180ac3e..d23b940` touches only comments and docs; `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` appears only as unchanged context in both units. Probe evidence (command, output, `uname -r`) is documented in the commit message.
3. **Checks** — `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` and `just lint` (vet + golangci-lint incl. e2e/realhosts tags) all green in the worktree. The new `install.md` §3b anchor resolves to the actual heading at `docs/install.md:499`.
4. **Residual inconsistency found** — `internal/bind/pathsock.go:31-33` (a surface the task description explicitly names: "any other CAP_NET_RAW mention in internal/bind"): the rewritten sentence reads "on Linux <5.7, SO_BINDTODEVICE needs CAP_NET_RAW **(the daemon runs privileged**, but the unit tests bind loopback unprivileged)". The parenthetical, now relocated inside the <5.7 clause by this diff, implies the daemon satisfies the pre-5.7 CAP_NET_RAW requirement — but the shipped units grant only CAP_NET_ADMIN, and `docs/install.md:879` + `pathsock_linux.go` state the opposite (pre-5.7 daemon EPERMs and falls back). This resurrects, in miniature, exactly the pathsock-comment-vs-units disagreement D40 exists to eliminate. Trivially fixable (e.g. "the shipped units grant only CAP_NET_ADMIN, so a pre-5.7 daemon falls back too; unit tests bind loopback unprivileged").

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T140 round 1 (diff 180ac3e..d23b940, worktree agent-a36797e065c1f835a)
- **Achieved:** verdict disapprove; 1 criticism / 0 questions
- **Discovered:** >=5.7 no-capability claim independently reconfirmed by a local zero-caps probe (kernel 7.0.8, `Current: =`); diff is comment/docs-only, bounding sets unchanged; build/vet/test/lint green; §3b anchor valid; probe evidence documented in the commit message
- **Issues:** none

```json
{
  "taskId": "T140",
  "verdict": "disapprove",
  "criticism": [
    "internal/bind/pathsock.go:31-33 — the diff relocates the pre-existing parenthetical '(the daemon runs privileged, but the unit tests bind loopback unprivileged)' inside the new 'on Linux <5.7, SO_BINDTODEVICE needs CAP_NET_RAW' clause, implying the daemon meets that pre-5.7 requirement. The shipped units grant only CAP_NET_ADMIN (not CAP_NET_RAW), and pathsock_linux.go + docs/install.md:879 state the daemon itself EPERMs and falls back on pre-5.7 kernels. The task description explicitly requires aligning 'any other CAP_NET_RAW mention in internal/bind'; reword the parenthetical so it does not suggest the daemon holds CAP_NET_RAW (e.g. note the units grant only CAP_NET_ADMIN, so a pre-5.7 daemon also takes the source-IP fallback; unit tests bind loopback unprivileged as well)."
  ],
  "questions": [],
  "defects": [],
  "rationale": "The >=5.7 rule, probe evidence, and unchanged CapabilityBoundingSet all verify (independently reconfirmed via a zero-capability local probe; build/vet/test/lint green; comment/docs-only diff). One in-scope surface — the pathsock.go CAP_NET_RAW mention the task text names — retains a parenthetical implying daemon-side CAP_NET_RAW availability that the other three surfaces contradict.",
  "summary": "Kernel claim, probe, and checks verified; one residual pathsock.go parenthetical still implies the daemon holds CAP_NET_RAW pre-5.7, contradicting the CAP_NET_ADMIN-only units — fix and re-review."
}
```
